text: What is the difference between a struct and a class in Swift?
coreKeyPoints: Structs are value types
Classes are reference types
Value types are copied on assignment
Reference types share a single instance
Classes support inheritance
Structs do not support inheritance
bonusKeyPoints: Structs get an automatic memberwise initializer
Classes use ARC
Mutating a struct property requires the mutating keyword
Reference types are compared with the identity operator ===
Classes can have deinitializers
Protocols and composition replace inheritance for structs

text: Explain how ARC manages memory and how retain cycles happen.
coreKeyPoints: ARC deallocates an object when its strong reference count reaches zero
A retain cycle is two objects holding strong references to each other
A weak reference does not increase the retain count
A closure capturing self strongly can cause a retain cycle
A [weak self] capture list breaks the cycle
bonusKeyPoints: A weak reference becomes nil when the object is deallocated
An unowned reference does not increase the retain count
An unowned reference assumes the object outlives it
ARC is compile-time, not a runtime garbage collector
Delegate properties are commonly declared weak

text: What is the difference between escaping and non-escaping closures?
coreKeyPoints: A non-escaping closure runs before the function returns
An escaping closure can be called after the function returns
An escaping closure is marked with @escaping
An escaping closure must capture self explicitly
bonusKeyPoints: Completion handlers are typically escaping
Non-escaping is the default
Non-escaping enables compiler optimizations
Stored closures must be escaping
Escaping closures can extend the lifetime of captured values

text: How does SwiftUI decide when to re-render a view?
coreKeyPoints: SwiftUI re-evaluates body when a source of truth changes
@State holds local view-owned state
@Binding passes mutable state without owning it
@Observable triggers updates when an observed property changes
bonusKeyPoints: SwiftUI views are value types
SwiftUI diffs the view tree and updates only what changed
Recreating a view is cheap because views are lightweight
Identity affects whether SwiftUI reuses or replaces a view
Environment changes can also trigger re-evaluation

text: Explain value semantics versus reference semantics.
coreKeyPoints: Value semantics means each copy is independent
Reference semantics means copies share the same instance
Value types avoid unintended shared mutable state
Reference types provide shared identity
bonusKeyPoints: Swift uses copy-on-write for standard library value types
Identity is compared with the === operator for reference types
Value types are safer across concurrency boundaries
Apple recommends preferring value types by default

text: What is the difference between async/await and completion handlers?
coreKeyPoints: Completion handlers pass results through escaping closures
async/await writes asynchronous code in a sequential style
Errors propagate with try in async functions
await suspends the function without blocking the thread
bonusKeyPoints: A Task provides structured concurrency
async/await reduces closure nesting
Tasks support cancellation
async let runs work concurrently
Completion handlers can be forgotten or called twice

text: How would you design a URL shortening service?
coreKeyPoints: The core is a mapping from a short code to a long URL
Short codes can be generated by base62 encoding an ID
Short codes can alternatively be generated by hashing the URL
A key-value store fits the short-code-to-URL lookup
Reads are cached because the workload is read-heavy
bonusKeyPoints: Hash-based codes require collision handling
A 301 redirect is permanent
A 302 redirect allows click tracking
Distributed ID generation avoids a single counter bottleneck
Expired links need a cleanup or TTL strategy
A CDN can serve redirects closer to users

text: How would you design a news feed like Twitter or Instagram?
coreKeyPoints: Fan-out on write precomputes feeds when a post is created
Fan-out on read assembles feeds at request time
Precomputed feeds are cached for fast reads
Posts and the social graph are stored separately
bonusKeyPoints: A hybrid model handles high-follower accounts
Media is delivered through a CDN
Feeds are paginated rather than loaded all at once
A ranking step orders posts beyond reverse chronological
Fan-out on write is expensive for users with many followers

text: How would you design a chat or messaging system like WhatsApp?
coreKeyPoints: WebSockets provide persistent connections for real-time delivery
Messages are queued for offline users and delivered on reconnect
Message history is stored in a database partitioned by conversation
Delivery and read status are tracked per message
bonusKeyPoints: A presence service tracks online status
Connection servers are separated from storage for scaling
Group chat fans a message out to multiple recipients
Message ordering is preserved per conversation
End-to-end encryption protects message contents

text: How would you design a rate limiter?
coreKeyPoints: Token bucket allows bursts up to a fixed capacity
Leaky bucket processes requests at a constant rate
Sliding window counts requests over a moving time window
Counters are stored in a fast shared store like Redis
bonusKeyPoints: Distributed limiting needs a shared counter across servers
Requests over the limit are rejected with HTTP 429
Fixed-window counters can spike at window boundaries
Limits can be scoped per user, per IP, or per endpoint
A Retry-After header tells clients when to retry

text: How would you design an image or video upload and storage service?
coreKeyPoints: Media is stored in object storage rather than a database
Only metadata and references are stored in the database
Presigned URLs let clients upload directly to storage
Content is served through a CDN
bonusKeyPoints: Media is processed asynchronously for transcoding
Thumbnails are generated in a background job
Large uploads use chunked or multipart upload
Video is delivered with adaptive bitrate streaming
Uploaded files are validated for type and size

text: Tell me about a time you handled conflict on a team.
coreKeyPoints: Describes a specific situation
States the task or responsibility involved
Describes the actions the candidate personally took
States the outcome or result
Mentions the other person's perspective
Mentions a lesson learned

text: Tell me about a time you failed and what you learned.
coreKeyPoints: Describes a specific situation
States the task or responsibility involved
Describes the actions the candidate personally took
States the outcome or result
Takes personal ownership of the failure
States a specific lesson learned

text: Describe a time you had to meet a tight deadline.
coreKeyPoints: Describes a specific situation
States the task or responsibility involved
Describes the actions the candidate personally took
States the outcome or result
Mentions how work was prioritized
Mentions communicating trade-offs to stakeholders

text: Tell me about a time you received difficult feedback.
coreKeyPoints: Describes a specific situation
States the task or responsibility involved
Describes the actions the candidate personally took
States the outcome or result
Describes a non-defensive reaction to the feedback
States a resulting improvement

text: Describe a time you led a project or took initiative.
coreKeyPoints: Describes a specific situation
States the task or responsibility involved
Describes the actions the candidate personally took
States the outcome or result
Clarifies the candidate's personal role versus the team
Mentions a leadership lesson learned
